title: Optics Bridge signature or quorum validation failure historical bridge incident

historical_exploit.attack_summary: Optics Bridge was attacked and ownership of the multi-signature wallet was transferred. cLabs engineer Tim Moreton said that the multi-signature permission of Optics, a cross-chain communication protocol on Celo, was replaced because someone activated the Optics recovery mode (recovery mode) on the Ethereum GovernanceRouter contract, which caused the recovery account to take over the Optics protocol and overwrite it. The original multi-signature permissions. Tim Moreton said that he believes that the funds on the current cross-chain bridge are not risky. Tim Moreton also said that the situation occurred within 15 minutes after cLabs expelled James Prestwich. The team is currently contacting James Prestwich to find a solution. The team is currently working to exit the recovery mode and restore the community's multi-signature governance. James Prestwich responded on Twitter that he had never had the right to activate the recovery mode and expressed regret for cLabs and Celo's damage to his reputation.
historical_exploit.attacker_profile: External attacker or unprivileged caller as described by the SlowMist incident row; exact actor permissions must be re-verified before reporting.
preconditions: A bridge, cross-chain router, gateway, wrapped-asset minter, liquidity pool, or validator/proof path accepts attacker-controlled message, proof, signature, transaction, deposit, configuration, or accounting input.
The accepted input releases existing bridge-held value, mints unbacked wrapped assets, miscredits a deposit, or lets a caller move assets beyond entitlement.
The target mapping must be reachable without relying on privileged key compromise, DNS/frontend phishing, social-account compromise, or pure rug-pull behavior.

value_path.asset_or_reward: bridge-held assets, wrapped assets, bridge liquidity, withdrawals, refunds, or backed cross-chain liabilities
value_path.how_value_was_extracted: Optics Bridge was attacked and ownership of the multi-signature wallet was transferred. cLabs engineer Tim Moreton said that the multi-signature permission of Optics, a cross-chain communication protocol on Celo, was replaced because someone activated the Optics recovery mode (recovery mode) on the Ethereum GovernanceRouter contract, which caused the recovery account to take over the Optics protocol and overwrite it. The original multi-signature permissions. Tim Moreton said that he believes that the funds on the current cross-chain bridge are not risky. Tim Moreton also said that the situation occurred within 15 minutes after cLabs expelled James Prestwich. The team is currently contacting James Prestwich to find a solution. The team is currently working to exit the recovery mode and restore the community's multi-signature governance. James Prestwich responded on Twitter that he had never had the right to activate the recovery mode and expressed regret for cLabs and Celo's damage to his reputation.
value_path.why_it_paid: The historical row describes real loss, attempted bridge theft, unbacked minting, unauthorized withdrawal, or bridge-accounting failure that can be useful only if BridgeIndex has a matching current live-value extraction path.

mapping.why_this_might_map: BridgeIndex exposes bridge deposits, bot-gas accounting, withdrawal creation/approval/execution, refunds, token/resource mappings, relayer voting, and proposal execution. This historical primitive is useful only if it maps to extracting current BNB/USDT ledger value or backed bridge liabilities from the deployed BridgeIndex system.

notes: SlowMist attack method: Multi-signature permission vulnerability.
Selected for scanned because: recovery-mode/multisig ownership transfer permission flaw
This seed is not proof that BridgeIndex is vulnerable; Workflow 7 must reject it unless the primitive maps to non-admin extraction of a current live ledger row.
